allergens.name: Peanuts
allergens.description: Legumes growing underground, distinct from tree nuts. Common trigger for severe anaphylaxis.

allergens.name: Tree Nuts
allergens.description: Includes almonds, cashews, walnuts, pistachios. Often causes lifelong allergies.

allergens.name: Dairy
allergens.description: Milk proteins (whey and casein). Common in children, can cause digestive and skin reactions.

allergens.name: Gluten
allergens.description: Proteins found in wheat, barley, and rye. Trigger for celiac disease and wheat allergy.

allergens.name: Shellfish
allergens.description: Includes crab, lobster, shrimp, clams, oysters. A common adult-onset allergy.

allergens.name: Eggs
allergens.description: Ovalbumin and other egg white/yolk proteins. Widely used in baking and binding.

allergens.name: Soy
allergens.description: Soybeans and derived products. Widely found in processed foods and sauces.

allergens.name: Fish
allergens.description: Finned fish (e.g., salmon, tuna, cod). Often distinct from shellfish allergy.

allergens.name: Wheat
allergens.description: A cereal grain containing gluten and other allergen proteins like amylase trypsin inhibitors.

allergens.name: Corn
allergens.description: Maize and maize derivatives. Can cause reactions to corn starch, syrup, or oil.

allergens.name: Sulfites
allergens.description: Preservatives used in dried fruits, wines, and processed foods. Can trigger asthma-like symptoms.

allergens.name: Food Dyes
allergens.description: Artificial coloring agents (e.g., Tartrazine, Red 40). Associated with hives and asthma.

allergens.name: MSG Sensitivity
allergens.description: Monosodium glutamate, a flavor enhancer. Can cause flushing, headaches, or sweating in sensitive individuals.

allergens.name: Latex-Fruit Cross-Reactivity
allergens.description: Allergy to proteins in latex that cross-react with fruits like avocado, banana, kiwi, chestnut.

allergens.name: Sesame Seeds/Oil
allergens.description: Common ingredient in tahini, breadings, and oils. Rising allergen globally.

allergens.name: Mustard Seeds/Oil
allergens.description: Seeds and oils from the mustard plant. Frequently used in Indian pickles and cooking.

allergens.name: Coconut
allergens.description: Fruit of the coconut palm. Classified as a tree nut by the US FDA, though botanically a drupe.

allergens.name: Curd/Yogurt (Casein)
allergens.description: Fermented dairy rich in casein protein. Casein is heat-stable and survives fermentation.

allergens.name: Tamarind
allergens.description: Sour pod fruit used for flavoring in South Asian cooking. Can cause rare systemic hypersensitivity.

allergens.name: Jaggery (Mold-related)
allergens.description: Unrefined cane sugar. Can harbor mold spores during storage, causing reactions in mold-sensitive patients.

allergens.name: Prawns/Crustaceans
allergens.description: Specific shrimp/prawn allergens (tropomyosin). Heavily featured in coastal Andhra cuisine.

allergens.name: Black Gram (Urad Dal)
allergens.description: Legume widely used in South Indian batters (idli, dosa, vada). Can cause IgE-mediated reactions.

allergens.name: Green Gram (Moong/Pesara)
allergens.description: Legume used to make Pesarattu. Cross-reactive with other legumes like peas and lentils.

allergens.name: Chili/Capsaicin (Irritant Flag)
allergens.description: Capsaicin is a chemical irritant in hot peppers that triggers non-allergic oral burning, flushing, and digestive upset.

allergens.name: Celery
allergens.description: Common root/stalk allergen in Europe, can cause severe reactions. Included for complete academic screening.

symptoms.name: Hives / Urticaria
symptoms.description: Raised, extremely itchy red bumps or welts on the skin.

symptoms.name: Localized Rash or Redness
symptoms.description: Redness or minor rash localized to one area of the skin.

symptoms.name: Itchy Skin (Pruritus)
symptoms.description: Generalized itching sensation without obvious rash or hives.

symptoms.name: Mild Lip/Eyelid Swelling
symptoms.description: Subtle swelling of the lips, eyelids, or face (angioedema).

symptoms.name: Sneezing
symptoms.description: Repetitive, sudden sneezing fits shortly after exposure.

symptoms.name: Runny/Stuffy Nose (Rhinitis)
symptoms.description: Nasal congestion or watery discharge from the nose.

symptoms.name: Itchy, Watery Eyes
symptoms.description: Redness, itching, and excessive tearing of the eyes.

symptoms.name: Mild Abdominal Cramps
symptoms.description: Minor stomach discomfort or cramping after eating.

symptoms.name: Nausea
symptoms.description: Feeling sick to the stomach or inclined to vomit.

symptoms.name: Mild Diarrhea
symptoms.description: Loose stools, occurring once or twice shortly after food consumption.

symptoms.name: Tongue Swelling (Mild/Localized)
symptoms.description: Slight swelling or tingling of the tongue.

symptoms.name: Metallic Taste in Mouth
symptoms.description: A distinct copper or metallic taste on the tongue, often an early sign.

symptoms.name: Excessive Salivation
symptoms.description: Increased saliva production or drooling.

symptoms.name: Tingling or Itching in Mouth
symptoms.description: Oral allergy syndrome (tingling of lips, palate, or throat).

symptoms.name: Cough (Dry/Occasional)
symptoms.description: A light, dry cough without wheezing or shortness of breath.

symptoms.name: Heartburn / Acid Reflux
symptoms.description: Burning sensation in the chest or throat due to irritants/spices.

symptoms.name: Difficulty Breathing
symptoms.description: Shortness of breath, chest tightness, or gasping for air.

symptoms.name: Wheezing / Stridor
symptoms.description: Whistling or high-pitched sound during breathing, indicating airway constriction.

symptoms.name: Throat Swelling / Tightness
symptoms.description: Feeling like the throat is closing up, voice sounds muffled.

symptoms.name: Difficulty Swallowing
symptoms.description: Inability or pain when attempting to swallow liquids or food.

symptoms.name: Skin Pallor / Sudden Paleness
symptoms.description: Sudden loss of color in the face/body, indicating circulatory compromise.

symptoms.name: Confusion / Disorientation
symptoms.description: Inability to think clearly, memory gaps, or confusion.

symptoms.name: Dizziness / Lightheadedness
symptoms.description: Feeling unsteady, faint, or spinny, indicating low blood pressure.

symptoms.name: Loss of Consciousness / Fainting
symptoms.description: Passing out or falling unconscious due to systemic shock (anaphylaxis).

symptoms.name: Weak or Rapid Pulse
symptoms.description: A thready, fast, or barely palpable heartbeat.

symptoms.name: Drop in Blood Pressure
symptoms.description: Severe hypotension causing extreme weakness or syncope.

symptoms.name: Severe Abdominal Pain
symptoms.description: Intense, debilitating abdominal cramps or sharp pains.

symptoms.name: Persistent Vomiting
symptoms.description: Continuous, uncontrollable vomiting shortly after ingestion.

symptoms.name: Severe Watery Diarrhea
symptoms.description: Frequent, heavy watery stools indicating systemic GI reaction.

symptoms.name: Blueness of Lips or Skin
symptoms.description: Cyanosis, indicating severe lack of oxygen in the blood.

symptoms.name: Chest Pain or Tightness
symptoms.description: Pressure, squeezing, or pain in the chest area.

symptoms.name: Hoarseness / Muffled Voice
symptoms.description: Sudden voice change, indicating swelling around vocal cords.

symptoms.name: Widespread Hives & Swelling
symptoms.description: Hives covering large areas of the body, accompanied by severe swelling.

symptoms.name: Feeling of Impending Doom
symptoms.description: An intense sensation of anxiety, panic, or fear that something fatal is happening.

symptoms.name: Slurred Speech
symptoms.description: Difficulty speaking clearly or articulating words, indicating neurological impact.

medicines.description: A class of over-the-counter medications that block histamine receptors. Typically used to relieve itching, hives, sneezing, and runny nose.
medicines.warning: Consult a healthcare provider before use. May cause drowsiness depending on the formulation.

medicines.description: A mild over-the-counter corticosteroid ointment applied directly to the skin. Used to reduce swelling, redness, and intense itching of localized contact rashes.
medicines.warning: For external skin use only. Do not apply to open wounds or near the eyes.

medicines.description: A simple over-the-counter sterile salt water spray used to flush allergens, dust, and excess mucus out of the nasal passages. Relieves rhinitis symptoms.
medicines.warning: Use distilled or sterile water if mixing rinses manually. Follow bottle directions.

medicines.description: High-potency, non-sedating histamine blockers prescribed by a doctor for long-term daily management of chronic allergic symptoms.
medicines.warning: Available only by prescription. A doctor determines if this is suitable based on clinical testing.

medicines.description: A portable emergency injection device containing epinephrine (adrenaline). It rapidly reverses severe airway obstruction, throat swelling, and drop in blood pressure during anaphylaxis.
medicines.warning: Prescribed to individuals with confirmed severe allergies. Must be carried at all times and administered immediately during a severe reaction before calling emergency services.

medicines.description: A long-term treatment (allergy shots or sublingual drops/tablets) administered under medical supervision to desensitize the immune system to specific allergens.
medicines.warning: Requires formal allergy diagnosis (e.g. skin prick testing) and is conducted over 3-5 years under close clinical observation.

doctor_note.disclaimer: Educational content for this academic project â€” not a substitute for an in-person medical evaluation.
doctor_note.mechanism: Allergic reactions are hypersensitivity responses triggered when the immune system mistakenly identifies harmless food proteins as threats. Specifically, IgE antibodies bind to the allergen, triggering mast cells to release inflammatory chemicals like histamine. This causes blood vessel dilation (redness, swelling), smooth muscle contraction (difficulty breathing), and skin nerve irritation (itching/hives).
see_doctor_bullets: You experience respiratory distress, throat constriction, or dizziness (anaphylaxis indicators).
Symptoms recur consistently after consuming specific foods or food families.
You require clinical diagnostic testing to confirm exact IgE-mediated triggers.
doctor_note.allergist_evaluation: An allergist visit typically involves: a clinical interview, a Skin Prick Test (SPT) where tiny drops of allergen extracts are introduced to the skin surface, an IgE Blood Test to quantify circulating antibodies, or an Oral Food Challenge (OFC) conducted under strict medical supervision.

medicine_guidance.disclaimer: Confirm with a doctor before using any medication. Dosages and brands are omitted for clinical safety.

stage_a.description: A class of over-the-counter medications that block histamine receptors. Typically used to relieve itching, hives, sneezing, and runny nose.
stage_a.warning: Consult a healthcare provider before use. May cause drowsiness depending on the formulation.

stage_a.description: A mild over-the-counter corticosteroid ointment applied directly to the skin. Used to reduce swelling, redness, and intense itching of localized contact rashes.
stage_a.warning: For external skin use only. Do not apply to open wounds or near the eyes.

stage_b.description: High-potency, non-sedating histamine blockers prescribed by a doctor for long-term daily management of chronic allergic symptoms.
stage_b.warning: Available only by prescription. A doctor determines if this is suitable based on clinical testing.

stage_b.description: A portable emergency injection device containing epinephrine (adrenaline). It rapidly reverses severe airway obstruction, throat swelling, and drop in blood pressure during anaphylaxis.
stage_b.warning: Prescribed to individuals with confirmed severe allergies. Must be carried at all times and administered immediately during a severe reaction before calling emergency services.

top_allergens.name: Peanuts

top_allergens.description: Includes almonds, cashews, walnuts, pistachios. Often causes lifelong allergies.

top_allergens.description: Milk proteins (whey and casein). Common in children, can cause digestive and skin reactions.

doctor_note.disclaimer: Educational content for this academic project â€” not a substitute for an in-person medical evaluation.
doctor_note.mechanism: Allergic reactions are hypersensitivity responses triggered when the immune system mistakenly identifies harmless food proteins as threats. Specifically, IgE antibodies bind to the allergen, triggering mast cells to release inflammatory chemicals like histamine. This causes blood vessel dilation (redness, swelling), smooth muscle contraction (difficulty breathing), and skin nerve irritation (itching/hives).
see_doctor_bullets: You experience respiratory distress, throat constriction, or dizziness (anaphylaxis indicators).
Symptoms recur consistently after consuming specific foods or food families.
You require clinical diagnostic testing to confirm exact IgE-mediated triggers.
doctor_note.allergist_evaluation: An allergist visit typically involves: a clinical interview, a Skin Prick Test (SPT) where tiny drops of allergen extracts are introduced to the skin surface, an IgE Blood Test to quantify circulating antibodies, or an Oral Food Challenge (OFC) conducted under strict medical supervision.

Specifically, IgE antibodies bind to the allergen, triggering mast cells to release inflammatory chemicals like histamine. This causes blood vessel dilation (redness, swelling), smooth muscle contraction (difficulty breathing), and skin nerve irritation (itching/hives).
see_doctor_bullets: You experience respiratory distress, throat constriction, or dizziness (anaphylaxis indicators).
Symptoms recur consistently after consuming specific foods or food families.
You require clinical diagnostic testing to confirm exact IgE-mediated triggers.
doctor_note.allergist_evaluation: An allergist visit typically involves: a clinical interview, a Skin Prick Test (SPT) where tiny drops of allergen extracts are introduced to the skin surface, an IgE Blood Test to quantify circulating antibodies, or an Oral Food Challenge (OFC) conducted under strict medical supervision.

stage_a.description: A class of over-the-counter medications that block histamine receptors. Typically used to relieve itching, hives, sneezing, and runny nose.
stage_a.warning: Consult a healthcare provider before use. May cause drowsiness depending on the formulation.

stage_a.description: A mild over-the-counter corticosteroid ointment applied directly to the skin. Used to reduce swelling, redness, and intense itching of localized contact rashes.
stage_a.warning: For external skin use only. Do not apply to open wounds or near the eyes.

stage_b.description: High-potency, non-sedating histamine blockers prescribed by a doctor for long-term daily management of chronic allergic symptoms.
stage_b.warning: Available only by prescription. A doctor determines if this is suitable based on clinical testing.

stage_b.description: A portable emergency injection device containing epinephrine (adrenaline). It rapidly reverses severe airway obstruction, throat swelling, and drop in blood pressure during anaphylaxis.
stage_b.warning: Prescribed to individuals with confirmed severe allergies. Must be carried at all times and administered immediately during a severe reaction before calling emergency services.

top_allergens.name: Green Gram (Moong/Pesara)
top_allergens.description: Legume used to make Pesarattu. Cross-reactive with other legumes like peas and lentils.

top_allergens.name: Chili/Capsaicin (Irritant Flag)
top_allergens.description: Capsaicin is a chemical irritant in hot peppers that triggers non-allergic oral burning, flushing, and digestive upset.

doctor_note.disclaimer: Educational content for this academic project — not a substitute for an in-person medical evaluation.
doctor_note.mechanism: Allergic reactions are hypersensitivity responses triggered when the immune system mistakenly identifies harmless food proteins as threats. Specifically, IgE antibodies bind to the allergen, triggering mast cells to release inflammatory chemicals like histamine. This causes blood vessel dilation (redness, swelling), smooth muscle contraction (difficulty breathing), and skin nerve irritation (itching/hives).
see_doctor_bullets: You experience respiratory distress, throat constriction, or dizziness (anaphylaxis indicators).
Symptoms recur consistently after consuming specific foods or food families.
You require clinical diagnostic testing to confirm exact IgE-mediated triggers.
doctor_note.allergist_evaluation: An allergist visit typically involves: a clinical interview, a Skin Prick Test (SPT) where tiny drops of allergen extracts are introduced to the skin surface, an IgE Blood Test to quantify circulating antibodies, or an Oral Food Challenge (OFC) conducted under strict medical supervision.
